author: @beamer.electronics
text: I've always found scrapyards exciting, but also find it sad that it's underused - great resource and thanks for promoting it, Rob. Example: Fuel pump replacement for my diesel = Mega Bucks, from a scrapyard = £15 and fitted it myself - worked for years.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: Can't do that here in Australia anymore. Work, Health and Safety put a stop to scavenging. Wreckers will bring the part to you at the front desk. It's hard to get stuff from scrap metal dealers as well. Last time I asked, government regulations stop them from selling to end users. That sucked when I could see some beautiful copper buss bars just sitting in a bin 1m away.

author: @docink6175
text: over here scrap metal and scrap cars are totally different yards and most scrap metal yards dont sell bits and flotsam.. If you take scrap in you can occasionally pick up something but its frowned upon.. Salvage yards for cars have changed greatly since my days of wandering and scavenging, now everything has its fuel tank and radiators removed then its sat on blocks without its tires in many cases

author: @AdricM
text:
Ive been doing the opposite,   been tasked with clearing out a warehouse of old  auction surplus rehears labs stuff.  There is so much potential going into the dumpster,  and off to one of our local scrap yards.  they pay us better for cleaned sorted stuff.  but really in the last 2 months we have netted over $6000 in USD  giving them the leftover cases, and copper, and aluminum bits.  we also have a ton of gold plated stuff (much of it a bit older) we may do a recovery class at our makerspace with.  ether way,  lots of goodies also went to the local makers, and our makerspace,  should keep us in kit and bits for a long time,  and some extra cash for lean times by ebay and selling some of it.   Cant wait for this to be done.  i like our local scrappers, but i would rather not be visiting them multiple times a day to drop off things.   
Cheers,  its good to remind folks that these places are everywhere,  not all are good for pickers.  but call around.

author: @jamesross1003
text: Scrap yards like that are the tinkerer's playground.  I love what you can find.  Before the pandemic there was a place at our local trash drop off where you could leave things or take them free of charge.  They called it "Take it or leave it".  It was great.  They had things like old washers/dryers,  HVAC window units, old tvs, microwaves, ect...  Was great for little projects.  Sadly, they have since not reopened it from the start of the covid mess.  One man's trash is another man's treasure.  I love going to places like that.  Thanks for the video Rob!

author: @stephenmorton8017
text: the problem with auto alternators is they require field coil current. a better choice is a DC permanent magnet motor from an old treadmill. better still is a smart motor from a washing machine. they have the advantage of being brushless and the three phases can be rewired to raise or lower the output voltage to your liking. here in good 'ol merica, scrap dealers are sullen, miserable characters that will more likely run you off and claim insurance issues and no bumbling fools allowed in the yard.

@markirish7599 yes, but most have a ratcheting hub and will only engage in one direction. they are brushless permanent magnet motors. there are vids about that.
some e-bikes regenerate.

I use Facebook marketplace and groups to find scrap now.

author: @boblewis5558
text:
It is truly sad that so many of the local council "recycling" centres WILL NOT UNDER ANY CIRCUMSTANCES allow anything to be removed from the bins etc. I have considered going and parking outside a couple of the ones in and around York with a board saying:

Scrapping White Goods? Please stop before entering and give your gear a second lease if life BEFORE scrapping it!

There should be a national scheme for doing this. However, I HAVE had a lot of success going to White Goods sales rooms. Many remove old washers etc on the sale of a new one. Some have their own small stockpiles until they reach the size when they do one trip to a recycler. Often having to PAY to actually remove them and may well be happy for you to take stuff.

It's the same with pallets. Halfords for example are quite happy for me to take any pallets from their little pile and also anything else sat there. It's worth just taking a trip around small industrial estates for skips. LOTS of opportunity to find useful stuff & free to boot. 

I have stripped several washers, tumble driers, dishwashers and the kind of "valuable" stuff includes:

Switches, wire, hose clips, synchronous and universal, powerful mains motors, drive belts, temperature sensors, stainless steel drums (fire pits), steel drive shafts, electronics components, heavy duty springs and for me, the most generically useful ... The flat, white, enamelled steel panels. Easily cut out to a basic rectangle with a cutting disk on an angle grinder. Then using a heavy duty metal guillotine (or the same angle grinder and deburring afterwards) cut to size and using a home made bending brake (scrap angle iron) make electronic enclosures. Two simple U channels (of dimensions to suit) and two front/rear panels with a few small jointing parts make brilliant enclosures! These  otherwise would easily cost North of £20-£30 PLUS!

author: @thefirstmissinglink
text: Most of our recycling facilities are one way unfortunately. So many of them don't want to deal with a liability of someone becoming injured while scouring their stock. Then it also complicates their bookkeeping then there is finding employees competent to manage transactions in both directions. My favorite place to go shopping has been the local landfill at a place that I live in Colorado.
